message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The number of patients of COVID-19 in Tokyo has been increasing gradually through the end of March, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Object</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Support for policymaking requires forecasting of the entire course and outcome of the outbreak if a self-restraint in going out is not initiated. Moreover, the effects of a self-restraint in going out must be considered when choosing to initiate one. Method: Data of Tokyo patients with symptoms during January 14 – March 28, 2020 were used to formulate a susceptible–infected–recovered (SIR) model using three age classes and to estimate the basic reproduction number (R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    ). Based on the estimated R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    , We inferred outbreak outcomes and medical burden if a self-restraint in going out were not enacted. Then we estimate the self-restraint in going out effects.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Results suggest R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    as 2.86, with a 95% confidence interval of [2.73, 2.97]. Exhaustion of medical resources can be expected to occur on April 26 if no self-restraint in going out occurs. If a self-restraint in going out were enacted from April 6, and if more than 60% of trips outside the home were restricted voluntarily, then medical care service could be maintained.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion and Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The estimated R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    was similar to that found from other studies conducted in China and Japan. Results demonstrate that a self-restraint in going out with reasonable cooperation of residents is required to maintain medical care.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Forecast of the COVID-19 outbreak and effects of self-restraint in going out in Tokyo, Japan
